one_line_takeaway: MalURLBench shows that 12 popular LLMs accept maliciously disguised URLs at rates from 32.9% to 99.9% purely based on URL text structure (before any page content is seen), and a cheaply QLoRA-fine-tuned 7B filter (URLGuard, trained on just 280 examples) cuts that risk by 81% on average, indicating the vulnerability is a knowledge gap rather than a fundamental capability limit.
key_finding: All 12 evaluated LLMs are substantially vulnerable to URL-structure-disguise attacks, with per-model overall risk score F(M) ranging from 32.9% (GPT-3.5-Turbo) to 99.9% (Mixtral-8x7B); other reported points include GPT-4o 38.2%, Llama-3-70B 35.7%, Qwen-Plus 35.7%, GPT-4o-mini 92.9%, Mistral-Small 98.2%, Llama-2-7B-chat-hf 95.2%, DeepSeek-Chat 84.7%, DeepSeek-Coder 41.7%, Llama-3-8B 50.6%, Mistral-7B 50.5% (Fig. 3b / Table 1). Larger active-parameter count correlates negatively with risk score (regression line, Fig. 3c). At matched/known active-parameter scale, MoE models (Mixtral-8x7B, DeepSeek-Chat) show higher F(M) than dense models (Mistral-7B, Llama-3-8B, DeepSeek-Coder, Llama-3-70B), attributed by the authors to expert-routing specialization amplifying training-data gaps around URL structures. 'Inducing' attacks (persuasive sentences embedded in URL) succeed more often (avg F(M)=71.5%) than 'imitating' attacks (decoy domain names embedded, avg F(M)=60.89%). Scenario risk (Fs) varies from a low (values partly obscured/out-of-range in three scenarios per Fig. 3d) up to Weather Information Assistant at 82.9% (highest), with mean 66.19% and std 9.17% across scenarios; sensitive-action scenarios (Food Delivery, Package Tracking) show comparatively lower risk. Shorter subdomain names (<=20 chars) yield higher F(M); beyond 20 chars, length shows no clear trend. Directory/parameter length shows no measurable effect on F(M). TLD identity matters: newer/rarer TLDs (.link, .art, .dev) yield high ASR (>80%), while common legacy TLDs (.com, .net, .world) yield low ASR. The mutation-optimization algorithm raised previously-underperforming templates substantially, e.g., GPT-3.5-Turbo on spkg rose from 0.0000 to 0.34 (+0.34), on scon from 0.01 to 0.23 (+0.22). URLGuard reduces risk score sharply across the 9 tested scenarios (average E=81%), e.g., scus 0.99→0.00 (−0.99), spkg 0.96→0.00 (−0.96), snew 0.94→0.00 (−0.94), svid 0.99→0.00 (−0.99), scon 1.00→0.14 (−0.86), sfood 0.94→0.05 (−0.89), sjob 0.98→0.01 (−0.97), swea 1.00→0.70 (−0.30, weakest), smus 1.00→0.61 (−0.39, second weakest); sshop was excluded from evaluation because its templates seeded training.
core_contribution: The paper introduces MalURLBench, the first benchmark for evaluating LLM-based web agents' vulnerability to maliciously disguised URLs at the 'stage 1' decision point (whether the agent's LLM chooses to trust/visit a URL before any page content is parsed). It contains 61,845 attack instances spanning 10 real-world agent scenarios (e.g., Package Tracking, Food Delivery, Weather Information, Job Search, Music Recommendation) crossed with 7 categories of real-world malicious websites (Phishing, Malware Injection, Fraud, Hacked Websites, Information Theft, Remote Control, Malicious Advertisement) sourced from public blocklists (FeodoTracker, SSLBL, URLhaus, ThreatFox, PhishingArmy, mitchellkrogza, firehol). Attack templates disguise the malicious payload in the URL's subdomain, path, or parameter field (e.g., 'videos-picked-just-for-you.[malicious].com'), generated via a manual-seed + GPT-4o expansion + GPT-4o merge pipeline, then iteratively improved with a custom 'mutation optimization' algorithm (Algorithm 1, inspired by Textual Gradient and Exemplar Optimization) that uses DeepSeek-Coder to analyze failed attack logs and mutate templates that fall below a per-scenario success threshold (Thd=10%). The paper evaluates 12 LLMs (GPT-3.5-Turbo, GPT-4o-mini, GPT-4o, DeepSeek-Chat/V3.1-Terminus, Qwen-Plus, Mistral-Small, Mistral-7B, Mixtral-8x7B, Llama-2-7B-chat-hf, Llama-3-8B, Llama-3-70B, DeepSeek-Coder) inside a MetaGPT agent wrapper with an explicit defensive system prompt, and analyzes which factors (model size, dense vs. MoE architecture, scenario, subdomain length, TLD type, attack semantics) drive attack success. As a defense, the authors build URLGuard: a QLoRA-fine-tuned Llama-2-7B-chat-hf acting as an isolated pre-detection filter, trained on only 280 labeled instances (140 malicious template variants from the sshop scenario expanded via DeepSeek-V3.1-Terminus, plus 140 benign URLs) held out from the scenario used at test time.
threat_model: Attacker controls only the textual content of a URL string (subdomain name, path, and/or query parameter) that is presented to an LLM-driven web agent; the SLD+TLD (i.e., that the destination is a known-malicious website) is fixed and taken from public malicious-website blocklists. The attacker's goal is purely to get the agent's LLM to output an 'accept'/trust decision on the disguised URL (stage 1 of the two-stage agent workflow: judge-then-visit-and-parse). The paper explicitly scopes out stage 2 (constructing or serving actual malicious webpage content) and does not build or host harmful pages; attack success is measured solely from the LLM's textual judgement output, not from real-world harm. Victims are the LLM/agent itself (and by extension its user/service provider) acting as the gatekeeper deciding whether to visit a link.
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: (1) scope is limited to URL structure manipulation (subdomain/path/parameter) and does not cover multimodal disguise (e.g., malicious URLs embedded in images/audio); (2) the threat model assumes attackers manipulate only subdomain/path/parameter fields, not more advanced techniques like dynamic URL generation or DNS-hijacking-based domain spoofing; (3) URLGuard is fine-tuned on a deliberately small, single-scenario-derived dataset (280 instances) specifically to demonstrate that base LLMs lack relevant knowledge (proof of concept P1), so its generalization to broader/more advanced attacks remains untested, and it already underperforms on 2 of 9 held-out scenarios (smus, swea) even within the paper's own test suite. Additional observations not flagged by the authors as limitations but evident from the text: attack success is measured purely from the LLM's textual accept/reject output under a single fixed defensive system prompt (HEXACO/Reid-Technique framing) — no evaluation of alternative or absent system prompts; the benchmark's 'ground truth' malicious websites derive entirely from existing public blocklists (no zero-day/undisclosed sites, by explicit ethical design choice), so results may not transfer to attacker-controlled fresh infrastructure; template generation and merging rely heavily on GPT-4o and DeepSeek models, which could introduce generator-specific biases into the attack template distribution.

datasets_used: MalURLBench (this paper's own benchmark): 61,845 attack instances, 10 scenarios, 7 malicious website categories

Public malicious-website blocklists used as website source: FeodoTracker, SSLBL (Abuse SSL Blacklist), URLhaus, ThreatFox, PhishingArmy, mitchellkrogza 'Big List of Hacked Malware Web Sites', firehol blocklist-ipsets

URLGuard training set: 280 labeled instances (140 malicious sshop-scenario template variants generated via DeepSeek-V3.1-Terminus + 140 benign URLs), manually annotated with malicious/benign rationale
quantitative_results: F(M) risk score range across 12 LLMs: 32.9% (GPT-3.5-Turbo) to 99.9% (Mixtral-8x7B)

GPT-4o F(M)=38.2%, GPT-4o-mini F(M)=92.9%, DeepSeek-Chat F(M)=84.7%, DeepSeek-Coder F(M)=41.7%, Llama-3-8B F(M)=50.6%, Llama-3-70B F(M)=35.7%, Mistral-7B F(M)=50.5%, Mistral-Small F(M)=98.2%, Qwen-Plus F(M)=35.7%, Llama-2-7B-chat-hf F(M)=95.2%
Average F(M) for inducing attacks = 71.5% vs. imitating attacks = 60.89%
Scenario risk Fs: mean 66.19%, std 9.17%; highest is Weather Information Assistant at 82.9%
Test volume per (model, scenario): 15 templates x 7 website categories x 3 sampled sites x 5 repetitions = 1,575 evaluations
URLGuard average defense effectiveness E = 81% across 9 tested scenarios (sshop excluded, used as training seed)

URLGuard per-scenario reduction examples: scus 0.99→0.00, sjob 0.98→0.01, snew 0.94→0.00, svid 0.99→0.00, spkg 0.96→0.00, sfood 0.94→0.05, scon 1.00→0.14, smus 1.00→0.61 (weakest, −0.39), swea 1.00→0.70 (weakest, −0.30)
Mutation optimization example gains: GPT-3.5-Turbo spkg template success 0.0000→0.34; scon 0.01→0.23
baselines_compared: 12 LLMs evaluated as the attacked systems: GPT-3.5-Turbo, GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, DeepSeek-Chat (DeepSeek-V3.1-Terminus), Qwen-Plus, Mistral-Small, Mistral-7B, Mixtral-8x7B, Llama-2-7B-chat-hf, Llama-3-8B, Llama-3-70B, DeepSeek-Coder

Defense baseline comparison is implicit: undefended LLM F(M) vs. URLGuard-filtered F(D(M)) for the same base model (Llama-2-7B-chat-hf), i.e. before/after QLoRA fine-tuning, not against other published defenses
